title: 2026/03/16 ENV (Part 3)
description: Monday, 16 March 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Te Hunga Roia Maori o Aotearoa Federated Farmers Te Arawa Lakes Trust
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Te Hunga Roia Maori o Aotearoa

chunks.text: Our next submitter, who is Te Hunga Roa Māori o Aotearoa. Welcome, Oscar, to the select committee this morning. Um, we have twenty minutes with you, so if you would like to, talk us through the main points in your submission, and then we'll open up for questions. So we'll hand over to you. Kia ora.

Ngā mihi, Catherine. I think Ms Katipo is going to kick us off, on behalf of Te Hunga, but... Æ, tēnā koutou e te komiti. Ko Kylie Katipo ahau. He uri ahau nō Waikato. Kei konei hoki a Oscar Wilson. He uri ia nō Ngāi Tahu, Ngāti Raukawa. Uh, Oscar and I are presenting the submissions on behalf of Te Hunga Roa Māori o Aotearoa in relation to the bills that are before this committee.

Te Hunga Roa Māori o Aotearoa is a society established in 1988 to represent the Māori legal community. Our vision is Mā te Ture mo te Iwi, by the law for the people. By way of introduction, Te Hunga Roa Māori acknowledges the need for reform of the resource management system. However, these bills, that are before you, as currently framed, risk diminishing Māori rights, undermining Te Tiriti o Waitangi and Treaty settlements, and marginalizing tikanga and mātauranga Māori in environmental management. More must be done to ensure that any reform

does not come at the expense of Māori rights. Enduring reforms require genuine partnership with Māori communities, co-designing a s-system that reflects our constitutional commitments. We support those who have already spoken, with the committee about the impact of the bill on Māori rights and Te Tiriti. And in our time with you this morning, we are going to focus on two kaupapa.

Firstly, Oscar will speak with you about Tiriti obligations and Treaty settlements, and then I will speak with you briefly around Māori participation rights in tikanga and mātauranga Māori. So without, further, further ado, I'll hand over to Oscar. Kei a koe e hoa. Mō tena koe Kylie, otirā, tena ra koutou nga kai komiti.

Um, as Ms Katipo's alluded to, I'll be speaking to the, the Hunga's concerns relating to Treaty settlements and Treaty-related obligations. Uh, while the bills propose a new framework for environmental management and spatial planning, they raise fundamental constitutional and legal concerns as to whether existing Treaty commitments are being upheld in substance.

Now, the bills purport to recognize Treaty settlement redress and existing participation arrangements but fail to provide the certainty, durability, and constitutional protection that Treaty settlements require. Settlement redress is treated as something to be adapted to the new framework rather than as binding constitutional commitments that the new system must accommodate and uphold. Essential concern for Te Hunga Roa Māori is the use of qualified and discretionary language, particularly the requirement that settlement redress be given effect only

to the greatest extent possible. Now, that formulation introduces uncertainty where certainty is essential. Treaty settlements are intended to provide durable redress for historical breaches of Te Tiriti, not to be reopened, weakened, or renegotiated through later legislative reform. Equivalence-based standards lower the enforceability of settlement arrangements and create a real risk that substantive rights will be eroded over time. The bills also impose rigid time frames within which the Crown and post-settlement government entities,

governance entities, pardon me, are expected to reach agreement on how settlement redress will operate under the new regime. These time-limited processes place a dipro-disproportionate pressure on Māori entities, many of which face ongoing capacity and resourcing constraints, and risk default outcomes if agreement cannot be reached in time.

If agreement cannot be reached, the bills risk, default outcomes that weaken settlement protections by default rather than mutual consent. There is also limited recognition for Māori with unsettled Treaty claims. By prioritizing settle, settled arrangements without equivalent safeguards for iwi and hapū whose claims remain unresolved, the bills risk entrenching inequities between Māori groups and undermining the Crown's obligation of active protection.

In our view, reform of this scale heightens rather than diminishes the Crown's responsibilities to those whose claims are ongoing. Taken together, the settlement provisions do not provide sufficient assurance that Treaty settlement redress will be upheld in substance within the new system. As drafted, the bills risk undermining the integrity and durability of negotiated settlements and exposing the Crown to further Treaty breaches.

These risks are compounded by the broader dilution of Treaty obligations across the bills. The repeal of Section six E, seven A, and eight of the Resource Management Act removes long-standing operative duties to recognize Māori relationships with the land, taonga, to have particular regard to kaitiakitanga, and to take into account Treaty principles. These provisions have long underpinned Māori participation in environmental decision-making and informed Treaty settlement outcomes. In their place, the bills introduced a narrowly

framed Māori interest goal and descriptive Treaty clauses that do not impose substantive system-wide duties on decision-makers. The Māori interest goal sits alongside other goals with no internal hierarchy, requires only that decision-makers seek to achieve them, and is subject to overriding provisions elsewhere in the bills.

This represents a material weakening of the legal s-- legal status of Treaty obligations and reframes Te Tiriti from a constitutional foundation into a contextual consideration. The cu-cumulative effect of these changes is to shift Māori involvement from partnership and shared authority toward constit-- consultation-based participation.Across both bills, Māori participation is primarily framed through procedural consultation requirements rather than decision-making roles or shared governance mechanisms. This approach reduce,

risks reducing Māori engagements to process compliance rather than meaningful influence, and is inconsistent with the principles of partnership, active protection and redress that underpin Te Tiriti and many treaty settlements. This dilution is also reinforced by the centralization of decision-making through national direction and ministerial intervention.

Once national instruments and environmental limits are set, opportunity for Māori influence at regional and local levels are significantly constrained. Often at these very stages is where Māori have the greatest and most enduring impacts on their decisions relating to whenua, wai and te taiao. Uh, the removal of an operative treaty clause also discards a well-developed body of jurisprudence that has guided decision-making under the RMA for almost three decades. The bills will also require local authorities to firmly understand the contents of any existing treaty

settlements in their takiwā, and traverse all pre-existing arrangements to ensure those arrangements will operate with the same or equivalent effect to the greatest extent possible under the new framework. In our view, this comes at a cost and is a role more appropriately carried out by the Crown as the treaty partner. Ultimately, the responsibility to reach outcomes that are consistent with Te Tiriti and consistent with treaty settlements rests with the Crown and cannot be treated solely as a matter for local authorities.

Whilst local authorities have an important role in the implementation, the bills should not rely on councils to resolve questions of equivalence, adaptation or priority in the absence of clear statutory direction. Without stronger protections and clearer allocation or responsibility, the current approach risks undermining the certainty, durability and constitutional integrity of treaty settlements, rather than giving effect to them as enduring commitments. Addressing these issues, in our view, requires the reinstatement of clear, enforceable, treaty related

duties that are at least equivalent to those under the RMA, and that operate as an effective check and balance on the exercise of statutory power. Overall, both bills significantly centralize decision-making power at the national level through ministerial intervention mechanisms and national direction instruments. While consistency is an articulated objective of the bills, this centralization reduces opportunities for Māori influence at regional and local levels, limits adaptability to local tikanga and mātauranga Māori, and risks

decon-- disconnecting decision-making from those most affected, being iwi, hapū and whānau. Once national direction is set, there are few opportunities for Māori to influence outcomes at later stages, and in our view, this top-down approach undermines the rangatiratanga and kaitiakitanga within rohe and takiwā. I'll whawhai the rākau back to Ms Katipo.

Tēnā koe, Oscar. So I'm going to touch on, a few different matters in relation to tikanga and mātauranga Māori and specific, aspects of the bill as I go through my section. So to begin with, in terms of tikanga and mātauranga Māori, the reforms do not establish, tikanga and mātauranga, mātauranga Māori as foundational elements of the resource management system.

Instead, they treat mātauranga Māori and tikanga as something to be consulted or considered, rather than a knowledge sy-system in and of itself, and that is, that has equal standing with Western scientific methodologies. The problem with this approach is that it limits the practical exercise of kaitiakitanga by iwi, hapū, whānau, Māori landowners. It fails to ensure that Māori concepts such as mauri, whakapapa or wāhi tapu are in, are integrated into the environment, environmental limit setting, effects assessment or

decision-making process, processes. And this fails to provide for the cultural landscape, which is, unique to Aotearoa New Zealand. This, of course, risks, as Oscar mentioned, ongoing litigation around uncertainty, and the inadequacy of the recognition of tikanga and mātauranga Māori.

Specifically in relation to identified Māori land, the definition of identified Māori land in the bill is also problematic. It's unduly narrow and doesn't align with the established frameworks for Te Ture Whenua Māori Act, which of course is, prefaced by the relationship of Māori with their whenua and, obligations arising between the Crown and Māori in relation to Te Tiriti. This definition of identified Māori land excludes land held for communal purposes, land subject to outstanding treaty claims, including under the Marine and

Coastal Area Takutai Moana Act, and land held by successor entities. It marginalizes Māori interests and limits kaitiaki responsibilities, which don't stop at, land boundaries. It's inconsistent with the Te Ture Whenua Māori Act definition of general land owned by Māori, which is not, restricted by, as it is in these bills, by orders of the court or previous provisions of the law, that might have applied to the change of that status. Um, this all places a narrowed focus on specific categories of land where Māori-- where the Māori,

view of te taiao is holistic.And of course, this doesn't align neatly. In relation to sites of significance, the bill also-- the bills also adopt a site-specific approach to recognizing, the sites of significance to Māori, focusing on discrete locations rather than broader cultural landscapes. It fails to reflect the holistic relationship between Māori and their whenua and the wai, and the te taiao broadly.

It does not provide for unconnected systems of whakapapa, tikanga, and customary practices. It excludes the recognition of cultural landscapes as discrete values or outcomes, weakening the protection outco- weakening the protections as compared to the existing RMA framework. It risks the loss of important values associated with cultural landscapes which are integral to both the, natural and cultural heritage.

We have concerns about the regulatory relief framework as well. Uh, that framework poses a significant risk to that councils will avoid identifying or protecting additional sites of significance due to potential financial and administrative relief obligation. It applies not only to newly identiful- identified sites, but also to those carried over in existing plans, which of course has the potential to lead to reassessment, that prioritize land over interest, landowner interests over Māori land, Māori cultural values. It risks undermining existing protections for

Māori cultural heritage and further entrenching barriers to recognizing sites of significance. And in relation to, Māori participation rights, as Oscar has, to build on what Oscar has already spoken to you about, the role of Māori is limited primarily to consultation rather than decision-making authority.

And for example, in relation to regional spatial planning, once those plans are finalized, Māori concerns not reflected at that stage are effectively locked out of subsequent processes. The bill does not require councils to consult with tangata whenua on appointing commissioners with tikanga or te ao Māori expertise. Uh, nor do they require such appointments, even where Māori interests are central.

And this, in our submission, perpetuates the decisions being made without appropriate cultural competence and a failure to uphold the participatory frameworks that are already established under the RMA. Uh, in summary, the reforms constrain, constrain Māori participation, diminish recognition of Māori rights in relation to land and sites of significance, and introduce new risks through the regulatory relief framework. And, I'll, I'll just pass back over to Oscar, who has a brief conclusion for us before we take, any pātai or questions that you have.

Ngā kui e hoa. I, in summary, Te Hunga Roa Māori o Aotearoa recommend, the strengthening of treaty clauses to require decision-makers to give effect to Te Tiriti. Uh, the broadening of definitions, of identified Māori land. Embedding tikanga Māori and matauranga Māori as foundational elements in all parts of the system. Uh, ensuring meaningful Māori participation at all stages, not merely just consultation.

And protecting treaty settlement redress without qualification or ar-arbitrary time frames. Uh, reform capable of commanding long-term legitimacy must be co-designed, constitutionally grounded, and supported by statutory provisions that give practical and enforceable effect to Te Tiriti.

Uh, that, that summarizes, our submission in whole and, yeah, we'll take any pātai now. Kia ora. Thank you very much for your very thorough submission. Uh, look, we will go to Lann Farm first for a question. Tēnā koutou. Thank you so much for that submission.

Um, I wanted to pick up on your comments about ministerial direction and centralized powers. And one concept that's come up from various submitters even today is taking the ministerial powers out of m- the minister's hands and basically putting them with a board of inquiry that might make recommendations to the ministers. Are you aware of any instances where board of inquiry processes have actually been able to, I guess, give due weighting and due

voice to iwi Māori, to settlement groups, that do you think there could be a way that we could make that system work where Māori rights and interests are actually, catered to at that high level? The pātai pai. Uh, hoya no, I'm, I'm not aware of any, situations where that has, eventuated or, you know, come to fruition. Um, there may, there may well be, but I'm not a-aware of that. I think fundamentally our, our biggest concern a-as Te Hunga Roa is for the, funnel approach that's, articulated in, in sections twelve of both of the bills in terms of

where the national policy direction sits, I guess, a-and takes precedence over national standards, spatial plans, land use plans. Uh, in our view, the, the spatial plans a-and land use plans are where, iwi and hapū a-and specific takiwā and rohe can make i-influential, decisions or, or assist in those decisions. Um, and without that ability, I suppose having that ability, sit beneath the national policy direction, which will inevitably apply more generally across Aotearoa, that's where our, our main concerns sit. Um, so whether that's, taking the

de-decision-making power away from the minister, specifically, I'm not so sure, and that probably needs a little bit more thought. But, yeah, that's where our concerns lie at the moment. Thank, thank you. Uh, Grant McCallum. Yeah, Kia ora. Uh, thank you for your submission. Um, I just wanna clarify something, 'cause I'm listening to your, your thorough submission is, like, I, I'm very conscious of the fact, and I'm a farmer myself, and a lot of number of farming com- families going through a reconsenting process so they can carry on farming.

And, and the, at the, at, particularly in Canterbury, they are, they are having to consult with the, and get the, basically get the approval of, of, of the local hapū and iwi. Is that where you envisage that process still continuing in that vein? Is that what you, how you see that should be happening or not?

Uh, tena koe. Um, I think it, it, it needs to happen at all levels is what we're saying. So there's, is value in a relationship, at every level, that engages the environment. And so we would see that engagement, with Māori, on at a, at a planning and consenting level is also fundamental to understand what might the particular, the particular significance of the land, its land use, and all of those things are encapsulated at that point.

Um, not just at, at a top point as well. So that's that, what Oscar was saying in terms of the funnel. Ma- the Māori voice needs to be consistent throughout every stage of the environmental management process in our submission. I- is, is that, is that part of your treaty-- is that actually part of treaty obligations in your view, at that level?

At the farming level, individual farming level? We would say yes. Mm. In, in r- in the, in the, in the r- holistic worldview for Te Ao Māori is that all things are interconnected, and that, whenua in particular, the interconnection of that whenua, it's important, that relationships are engaged even at that primary level.

Thank you. Look, we have met time. We appreciate your time. Thank you very much, for that very thorough insight. Um, you know, some valuable considerations there, so appreciate your time. Thank you. Tena koutou. Tena koutou. Kia ora. So we now move on to our next submitter, Federated Farmers.

And we can see you there, Wayne. Hi. And Paul. And if we got, someone else joining us as well. Welcome both to-... the select committee this afternoon. Uh, you have 20 minutes with us. Uh, we've got your very weighty submission here. Um, obviously a lot to traverse, but look, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through the main points, and then we'll open it up for questions.

Great. Thank you, Catherine, and, thank you everyone for your time. Um, it's great to have the opportunity to appear here today. Um, as mentioned, my name's Wayne Langford. I'm the current president of Federated Farmers. I'm a Golden Bay dairy farmer. And to be fair, I'm not overly impressed with the bill as it stands. Uh, joining me today I have our RMA spokesperson, Mark Hooper, a dairy farmer in Taranaki, Paul Melville and Kelly Langdon from our, expert policy team at Federated Farmers.

Now, the principles, principles of the reform to begin, you know, I wanna make it really clear that Federated Farmer, Farmers, sorry, supports the intention of these reforms. Uh, we support the objective of enabling the primary sector growth. We support a system based on the enjoyment of property rights.

Uh, we support narrowing the scope of the effects. We support a greater reliance on the standards that, standards and farm plans, and we definitely support reducing the number of expensive consents. Those are all great things to be worth, working towards, but unfortunately, the legislation as it currently is drafted won't deliver them. And my concern is that if this bill were to pass in its current form, it would be significantly worse for farmers, than the current RMA.

Now, from a farmer perspective, I'll, I'll bring it down to my, my farm, 'cause that's where I, where I am probably a bit more of an expert. Uh, and, and I'll let the probably the, the policy team, Paul and, and Kelly cover off a few more questions during question time, but, like the committee, I've, I've been through the 700-page, document.

Uh, it's, it's a fair bit of a read and it's somewhat a fair bit for a farmer like me to get their head around. But when I'm reflecting back home to my own situation here in Golden Bay, my wife and I, Tyler, we farm 240 dairy cows. We've got 125 hectare, dairy farm, of which about 35 hectares of that is, is a native bush and, and, retired bush.

Uh, we run a really low stocking rate system. A quarter of our farm, li- like I mentioned, is in, in native diversity. Uh, our catchment is an, is an A catchment band. Uh, and I have a farm environment plan like, many of the other dairy farmers around the country. Uh, this plan contains all the actions I'm taking on my farm to continue to improve the environmental performance.

Now, I don't currently need a resource consent on my farm, and by all measures my farm represents what most urban Kiwis want farming to look like. Uh, I'll mention it, it, it has been on Country Calendar, which is, which I think was just celebrated a f- a few weeks ago as well, and, and got quite a good response there. So what will the Natural Environment Bill mean, for me?

Under the Natural Environment Bill, my local Tasman Council, will need to set an environmental limit for fresh water. Now, I, I already know what that limit will be because the local catchment is subject to water conservation order, or a WCO. Under that bill, that, this will prevail over any national or regional rule, meaning my catchment will, will immediately be beyond the environmental limit.Uh, Clause 32 states that because our catchment is beyond the environmental limit, my land use should be a discretiona- my end...

Sorry, my land use should be a discretionary activity. This will mean spending tens, if not hundreds of thousands of dollars on reports to get a consent to farm. And even after going through all, all, all of that, I still not ma- ma- I still might not even be able to get that consent. Now, Clause 85 states that no national standards can be set, for activities as I do my catchment breaches a- and the environmental limits. So I'll also need a resource consent to maintain drains, to build a wetland, or to change land use.

Something that's, you know, I consider really important to me, particularly when I've got three young sons who might be coming home to the farm. The staged intervention approach of the bill also means I may be subject to input controls down the line, which is really serious stuff as well. At best, I'll need to spend tens of thousands of dollars on consultants under the bill to work through that as well.

At worst, I lose my... I, I lose the right to farm, and I face walking off the land. I lose that valuable production and the valuable resource into which we provide, for not only the community, but New Zealand. So if I say that again, if I can't get a consent on my farm, I can't farm and I'll need to walk off, off the farm. In a catchment that is 100%... It has, sorry, 100% swimmable waterways, has an outstanding effluent management compliance rate of well over 95%. For farmers across the country, that means the loss of

livelihood and their life's work. For banks, it means mortgages not paid, and for rural communities, it means more ghost towns, as we're seeing with, you know, significant areas of forestry and stuff around the country. And I, I, I put it to you probably and I ask you, you know, what for? What are we trying to achieve that for? And is, is that achieving the goal of what we're setting out to do? So I'll now pass on to, to Mark Hooper, who's lead this discussion for Farmland Farms and spokesperson for RMA.

Mark, I'll hand to you. Hi, Wayne. It's Paul Melville here. I think Mark is having some technical details, so I'll just read through the, um- Just... parts that Mark was gonna do. Are you there, Mark? Uh, yeah. Can, can anyone hear me? We can hear you. Yeah. Uh, can you see me?

No, we can't see you. Okay. That, that might be where the problem is. We'll just, run with that. I'll just continue to, with our presentation then verbally. Uh, so Wayne's farm might be considered unique because he has a WCO, but it's, it's likely that this will actually be the situation for virtually all farmers in New Zealand under this bill.

We believe that virtually all communities in New Zealand will want to set standards for water quality that improve water beyond the current levels. And as soon as they do this, by definition, they are breaching an environmental limit. As such, A, all farms will need a resource consent, B, they will struggle to get a consent, and C, it won't be possible to use national standards for farming activities. And this situation, of course, goes completely against Cabinet's intent of having less resource consents, relying more on national standards

and enabling primary sector growth, and this is a fundamental flaw in the design of the legislation. So what is the way forward? Well, we think that the bill needs to be reframed. All areas of New Zealand should be able to set targets, if they wish, that improve water quality. But this shouldn't mean that all activity stops until that target is achieved.

As a, an a- analogy on this, consider air quality in our cities. If we want to improve air quality, we understand that we can do this by setting standards for motor vehicles that improve performance over time, but we don't ban all new cars, and water quality is the same. We can improve water quality by improving the performance of all farmers over time, and we don't need resource consents to do this. We have a tool already, farm plans. Farm plans can allow all farmers to improve performance over time, improving subsequently the

water quality over time. We can also do this by setting standards for farming activities like drain maintenance, vegetable growing, aerial top dressing, et cetera. As each catchment will have a different target, standards need to flex up or down for catchment context, which is a feature that's already built into farm plans. I wanna finish that we have, substantial changes outlined in our submission on other parts of the bill to address broader concerns. Uh, firstly, and in particular, changes to the goals, the definition of effect and processes for

setting environmental limits are covered. These changes are aimed at making sure councils are able to make informed trade-offs when setting environmental limits. Uh, secondly, that the scope of the bill is constrained to physical effects on the natural environment, including, spec... or, sorry, specifically excluding greenhouse gas emissions and spiritual impacts such as the mana of the water. We also have strong concerns on the degree to which the bills may allow many policy decisions to be made by ministers through secondary

legislation, concerns about mechanisms to auction natural resources such as fresh water, and also concerns about the broad nature of provisions for living natural resources such as fresh water. Wayne? Yeah. Thanks, Mark. So to wrap up, I mean, we support the, reform objectives of enabling primary sector growth while protecting and enhancing the environment. But to deliver them, the bill needs substantial changes.We have outlined some key concerns with the Natural Environment Bill in the, the limited time that we've had here

today. Our submission contains further detail, on both the National Environment Bill and the Planning Bill. If we get the architecture right, we can have both the environmental improvement and economic growth. But if we get it wrong, we suddenly risk just rebuilding the RMA, but potentially even worse and significantly worse for farmers. And so now I'll open up to questions. I'm happy to take on any questions.

Thanks, Catherine. Yeah. Thank you, Wayne, for that, and obviously putting into context your own practical farm, experience there as well. So, look, we've got a couple of questions. So we'll go first to Steve Abel. Kia ora. Thank you. Thank you. Um, so many questions, but, you mentioned the, problem of Maori land loss at one part in your submission in terms of the impact of confiscation.

I wonder why you're so against the recognition of sites, wahi tapu, and sites and, of biodiversity significance to iwi Maori, whilst you recognise, much of the land that will be protected by this legislation in terms of private property rights is confiscated land, i.e., it's the very land that you refer to as having been, stolen effectively and having property rights removed from iwi Maori.

Um, and then in the same breath, you're saying but we shouldn't recognize that, Maori have interests and concerns about, the likes of biodiversity and significant sites and mauri of water, which is a, you know, a cultural value as well as being an ecological value.

Uh, so, so what... Sorry. Exactly what's the question there, Steve? So with... In one breath you say private property rights are important and we understand how important they are because of the loss of private property rights in our history through colonization of Maori. And then in the other breath you say, "We don't want to see Maori, values recognized in terms of mauri, in terms of biodiversity, in terms of wahi tapu."

Oh, yeah. I can jump in if you like. Sorry. Yeah, okay. Go, Paul. Yeah, I think, I think, like we, we talk about the importance of property rights and how property rights haven't always been respected in New Zealand's history, and most famously through land confiscations from Maori. Um, I guess you have a second issue there where a lot of the overlays that come through, the RMA for things like outstanding natural landscapes, sites of significance to Maori and outstanding natural landscapes have the effect of taking property from farmers for the public

good. Now, because land was taken in the past, I wouldn't say that therefore two wrongs make a right, and kind of taking this land again now in the, in the, in the present is kind of, you know, due, due justice because you've obviously-- we don't typically approach treaty settlements that way. Treaty settlements are between the Crown, not between private landowners and Maori. And then at the same time, y- I think there's an argument there that these takings could be considered in the public good, so maybe they should be allowed to occur. And we would say, "Yes, they should be allowed to

occur." Just like land can be taken for power lines or motorways or gas lines and all these things. We accept that sometimes the government needs to take land from farmers or from other landowners for the public good, but we also always, under the Public Works Act, have a compensation. We don't say, "Oh, well, your house happens to be in the way of the motorway.

It's for the public good. We're sorry." We, we say, "Well, actually the Crown's gonna compensate you for that loss." So this is what we've said in our submission, and I think the intent of government is to do the same. They've outlined four specified topics which would be subject to a compensation regime. Um, and, and we, we kind of support that direction of travel.

We've got some comments in our submission around how we think that mechanism could be improved to make sure that there's less, kind of litigation involved in the process. And yet, I mean, I guess the other thing just to touch on is that, yes, it's fair for the farmer to not have to, um... or the homeowner who might be in the way of a motorway or, also an SNA. But it's also efficient because what we tend to find is that when councils don't have to pay for, outstanding natural landscapes and the other, it's very easy to protect almost

everything, you know, up to 40% of some districts protected under an ONL. And you've, and we've seen cost benefit assessments in Wellington, for example, where the cost of these overlays are more than the benefits that, that have been assessed. But it's very easy to do it if, if the benefits fall on the community and the cost is someone else. So where, where you have a compensation regime, it also provides a true cost benefit test for the council.

Yep, we might wanna protect some outstanding natural landscapes and some areas of historical significance to Maori, but we're gonna mainly focus on those that we can justify, because if we just protect everything, well, then we're gonna be taking on more costs than we can justify. Can I just clarify that- Um, sorry... Madam Chair with a sup? Um, yeah. Yeah, one sup. Just a quick one. So you're interpreting bit of significant biodiversity in terms of wahi tapu as a land take.

Uh, well, it's a regulatory taking in that if I have power lines over a farm, there's certain things I can't do under that, under those power lines. There's, there's limits on the activities, and there's also an impact on the value of the farm, and we find exactly the same thing with something like an ONL or an SNA. It's gonna limit what you can do on that farm and, and on that land, and it's also gonna reduce the value of that land.

But given the massive impact that your industry's had on biodiversity and that it still has on the commons in terms of fresh water, you're now gonna further punish the wider community and the commons by expecting it to pay to protect the little biodiversity we have left. So when you talk about the massive impact that farming's had on biodiversity, I think, there's more biodiversity on farms outside of the national park than, um... And, and, and the biodiversity on farms is, I can't remember off the top of my head, but I think it's larger than Mount Egmont National

Park. So there's quite a... I'm not su- I don't think I agree that farmers are having a big impact on biodiversity.You deny that farming has a massive impact on Look, thank you. Thank you, Steve. We're gonna leave it there 'cause we've got other questions from other members. Um, Cameron Luxton. Thank you, Chair. And it's kind of, it is a bit of a sup- I mean, Feds has acknowledged the importance of Wai tapu and wahi tap, tupuna on sites, but I mean, for farmers who are just trying to get on with farming, like considering

And it also says, all persons under this act have a duty to consider adverse effects. It could be human health impact, like we've had examples of McDonald's having to prove for a consent in Wanaka that it's not gonna impact human health. So impacts on water quality, air quality, biodiversity, et cetera.

And so that's the key, I think, is that going forward, farm plans need to be, they need to be developed in such a way that they identify the relative risk that's, that's, that's faced in that catchment. E ngā mana, e ngā reo, e ngā rangatira mā mō, mō te-- o, o te committee tēnei. Uh, tēnā rā koutou katoa. Uh, and thank you for the opportunity to present today on behalf of Te Arawa Lakes Trust. Uh, my name is Wally Haumaha. I'm the Chair of the Trust, which is the oldest Māori trust board in the country that was formed in nineteen twenty-four.

So we've been around a long, long time. I would like to begin with the words that sit at the heart of our relationship with the Crown, the Crown's apology and the Te Arawa Lakes Settlement of, Settlement Act of two thousand and six. In that apology, the Crown acknowledged that its actions undermined the rangatiratanga of Te Arawa over our lakes, and that those actions caused significant prejudice to Te Arawa and our ability to exercise our responsibilities as kaitiaki. The Crown expressed its regret and its commitment to restore the relationship and atone for those wrongs.

Those words matter deeply to our people. But as we consider the proposed reforms to the resource management system, Te Arawa Lakes Trust is concerned that we may once again be facing legislative change that risks repeating the very failures the Crown apologized for. The Lakes Trust is the statutory owner of the beds of fourteen lakes in the Rotorua district, and through our settlement, we share government's responsibilities for the health and restoration of those lakes. Our submission to this committee is grounded in one

central question: Will the new resource management system preserve the settlement arrangements that Parliament has already recognized and enacted? Moreover, will these proposed changes protect the lakes of Te Arawa from further degradation? As currently drafted, the bills risk reducing treaty settlement arrangements to goals that sit alongside competing development objectives rather than binding commitments that must be upheld.

That would fundamentally alter the balance struck in the settlement. We want to be clear, Te Arawa supports the need to improve and streamline the RMA. Our communities need a system that enable infrastructure, housing, economic development, but efficiency must not come at the cost or the expense of our environment, of our taiao.

For Te Arawa, the health of our lakes is inseparable from the well-being of our people and the prosperity of our region through tourism, recreation, culture. Local livelihoods will depend on the health of these waters. Strong economy and healthy environment are not competing objectives. They depend on each other. Te Arawa Lake settlement recognized our ownership of the lake beds and established governance arrangements to restore and protect these lakes for future generations. Those arra-ra-arrangements are not policy

preferences, they are legislated commitments to the Crown-- of the Crown. If the Crown chooses to redesign the res-- the RMA, then the protections that underpin our settlement must travel with that reform. In simple terms, the new system must deliver the same or equivalent legal effect for our settlement as the current Resource Management Act framework provides today.

Anything less risks undermining the integrity of treaty settlements themselves. Te Arawa Lakes Trust, therefore, supports reform that improves efficiency, but reform must strengthen, not dilute, the mechanisms that protect our taiao and honor the Crown's commitment under Te Tiriti o Waitangi.

The key issues and concerns the proposed bills as drafted do not guarantee the preservation of the Te Arawa Lakes settlement, our statutory lake bed ownership, or the co-governance arrangements and decision-making frameworks established by law. Settlement-derived rights risk being subordinated to development objectives, and treaty obligations are reduced to procedural engagement rather than substantive protection. There is no explicit embedding of settlement-specific governance, no guarantee of represente-representation for Te Arawa in statutory

bodies, and no protection for unsettled hapū within collective arrangements. These changes threaten to transform binding agree, settlement rights into discretionary matters, which is consistent with the constitutional status of treaty settlements. And the importance of structural protectio-protection for Te Arawa Lakes settlement resolved long-standing grievances and legally vested the lake beds in Te Arawa alongside statutory co-governance and Crown obligations for restoration.These are legislated outcomes and not simply symbolic recognition or cultural

acknowledgments. Any new regime for environmental limits or planning must not erode these legal rights or subordinate them to general policy goals. Structural protection must be embedded in primary legislation and national direction, not left to future negotiation or regional discretion.

Te Tua Papa is the integrated environmental and cultural framework for Te Arawa's exercise of Kaitiakitanga and settlement rights. It must be recognised as a statutory policy statement with binding effect, not ordinary. Failure would undermine both the legal framework and the practical restoration efforts made by Te Arawa. The settlement established shared outcomes, not just consultation. Te Arawa must have guaranteed structural representation in all statutory decision-making affecting our lakes, including spatial planning, environmental limit

setting, and national direction. This representation must be permanent and not left to the discretion of central or regional government. Our recommendations therefore, Te Arawa Lakes Trust urges the committee to ensure that settlement rights, propriety interests, and governance arrangements are explicitly and permanently protected in primary legislation.

Te Tua Papa o Ngā Wai o Te Arawa is recognised as a statutory policy statement with binding effect. Te Arawa representation in decision-making is guaranteed, not discretionary or consultative. Transitional or time limit, limited mechanisms are avoided. Protections must be permanent, structural, and not subject to renegotiation or inquiry. Both acts explicitly recognise the Te Arawa Lakes Settlement Act 2006, the Rotorua Te Arawa Lakes Strategy Group, and the statutory role of Te Arawa Lakes Trust, Trust. So in sum, we

seek that treaty settlement rights are structurally and permanently protected within the new RMA system with explicit priority and directive effect. This is set-- is essential to maintain the integrity of our two thousand and six settlement and the Crown's obligations under Te Tiriti o Waitangi. Nō reira, tena koutou katoa.

Those are my opening statements. Thank you. And any hard questions, I will refer to my technicians on the side of me. Kia ora, thank you for that submission. Um, and I think, too, around that, it's not-- we've-- we believe the Te Arawa Lakes Trust, or our Settlement Act is not an ordinary statute. You know, it gives legis-legislative effect to a negotiated settlement, of our taonga. So, you know, specifically and to and for our lakes.
